title: Fate of the Ising universality class under nonreciprocal interactions
authors: M. Akritidis, A. Garcés, A. Vasilopoulos, M. Carosi, D. Levis, N. G. Fytas
abstract: We study the critical behavior of a two-dimensional Ising model with nonreciprocal vision-cone interactions, which explicitly violate reciprocity and detailed balance. Extensive Monte Carlo simulations and dynamic renormalization-group analysis show that the asymptotic critical exponents remain fully consistent with the equilibrium Ising universality class over a broad range of nonreciprocal coupling strengths $λ$. In contrast, dimensionless quantities such as the Binder cumulant and the correlation-length ratio display pronounced anisotropic nonequilibrium corrections and systematically deviate from their equilibrium Ising values. The renormalization-group flow further demonstrates that the nonreciprocal perturbation is irrelevant at the Wilson-Fisher fixed point while generating a finite shift of the critical temperature proportional to $λ^2$. Our results demonstrate the remarkable robustness of two-dimensional Ising criticality against this class of directional interactions.
summary: The paper examines the stability of the 2D Ising critical point when subjected to nonreciprocal interactions that break detailed balance. Using a combination of Monte Carlo simulations and Renormalization Group theory, the authors demonstrate that while these NR terms cause measurable deviations in scaling ratios, they do not alter the fundamental asymptotic critical exponents. This highlights the remarkable robustness of equilibrium universality classes.
main_problem: The study investigates how the critical behavior of a two-dimensional Ising model is affected by nonreciprocal (NR) interactions that violate detailed balance.
main_result: The asymptotic critical exponents remain robustly consistent with the equilibrium 2D Ising universality class, indicating stability against these directional perturbations. However, dimensionless quantities show systematic deviations from their equilibrium values.
method: The research employs extensive Monte Carlo simulations, finite-size scaling analysis, and Renormalization Group (RG) techniques to analyze critical exponents and scaling corrections.
model_or_system: The model is a 2D Ising system on a square lattice subjected to nonreciprocal vision-cone interactions. The dynamics are governed by Glauber dynamics, leading to an effective Hamiltonian that lacks global reciprocity.
key_observables: Magnetization ($m$), Susceptibility ($\chi$), Binder cumulant ($U_4$), Correlation-length ratio ($\xi/L$), and the critical temperature shift $T_c(\lambda)$.
important_parameters: The nonreciprocal coupling strength $\lambda$ is varied across a broad range, while the reciprocal coupling $J$ sets the baseline equilibrium case.
main_assumptions: Analysis relies on fitting data to specific scaling forms and RG calculations are performed at one-loop order using momentum-shell procedures.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate finite-size scaling analysis of dimensionless quantities like $U_4$ and $\xi/L$, showing their dependence on the NR coupling strength $\lambda$. Other figures show the behavior of energy-like observables ($e$) at criticality.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from analyzing the lattice model using MC simulations to performing detailed RG calculations in momentum space. The structure builds by first establishing the stability of critical exponents, then quantifying the deviations in scaling ratios, and finally deriving flow equations for the couplings.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous theoretical framework for understanding how microscopic violations of time-reversal symmetry or detailed balance affect macroscopic phase transition universality classes, which is crucial for non-equilibrium statistical physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Finite-size scaling extrapolation of the crossing values of (a) the Binder cumulant U4 and (b) the correlation-length ratio ξ/L for pairs of system sizes (L, 2L). The horizontal dot-dashed lines indicate the corresponding universal values of the equilibrium 2d Ising model.

FIG. 1. Finite-size scaling analysis of (a) the crossing temper- atures T∗of the Binder cumulant U4 for system sizes (L, 2L), together with effective estimates of (b) 1/ν obtained from ratios of dU4/dT at the crossings, (c) β/ν from the magneti- zation m, and (d) γ/ν from the susceptibility χ. The vertical axis in panel (a) has been normalized by the corresponding ex- trapolated value of Tc. In panels (b)–(d), the horizontal dot- dashed lines indicate the exact values of the 2d Ising model. Panels (b)–(d) are shown together with simultaneous joint fits to the finite-size scaling forms discussed in the text.

FIG. 3. Finite-size scaling behavior of (a) the energy-like observable e and (b) the specific-heat-like fluctuation quantity C at the corresponding critical temperatures. Panel (a) is shown together with simultaneous joint fits for the exponent combination d −1/ν.
FIG. 4. Fit of the critical temperatures obtained from MC simulations and reported in Table I, using the RG prediction T (λ) c /T (0) c −1 ∼λ2.
